title: Ataxic Disorders
authors: Roger N. Rosenberg, Vikram G. Shakkottai

items.subtopic: Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA
items.title: Distinguishing True Cerebellar Ataxia from Vestibular Causes
items.content: True cerebellar ataxia is characterized by unsteady gait without vertiginous complaints, unlike vestibular nerve or labyrinthine disease which involves dizziness and light-headedness. Sensory disturbances can mimic cerebellar imbalance, worsening with visual input removal (Romberg sign).
keyPoints: True cerebellar ataxia lacks vertigo.
Unsteady gait due to imbalance is a hallmark.
Romberg sign indicates sensory ataxia.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA

items.subtopic: Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA
items.title: Etiologies of Acute and Reversible Ataxias
items.content: Acute and reversible ataxias can result from intoxication with substances like alcohol, phenytoin, lithium, barbiturates, toluene, gasoline, glue, spray painting, methyl mercury, bismuth, or cytotoxic drugs such as fluorouracil and paclitaxel. Postinfectious syndromes, particularly after varicella, can also cause reversible gait ataxia and mild dysarthria.
keyPoints: Acute ataxias may be due to intoxication with alcohol, phenytoin, lithium, barbiturates, toluene, gasoline, glue, spray painting, methyl mercury, bismuth, fluorouracil, or paclitaxel.
Postinfectious syndrome post-varicella can cause reversible gait ataxia and dysarthria.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA

items.subtopic: Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA
items.title: Paraneoplastic Cerebellar Ataxias and Associated Tumors
items.content: Paraneoplastic cerebellar ataxia can be associated with various tumors such as breast, ovarian cancers (anti-Yo), small-cell lung cancer (anti-PQ-type voltage-gated calcium channel), Hodgkin's disease (anti-Tr), and neuroblastoma. These syndromes may present as the initial symptom of cancer.
keyPoints: Breast and ovarian cancers associated with anti-Yo antibodies.
Small-cell lung cancer linked to anti-PQ-type voltage-gated calcium channel antibodies.
Hodgkin's disease associated with anti-Tr antibodies.
Neuroblastoma can present with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA

items.title: Acute Focal Ataxia: Recognize Neurological Emergencies
items.content: Acute focal ataxia often results from cerebrovascular events like ischemic infarction or cerebellar hemorrhage. These conditions can lead to severe complications, including brainstem compression and increased intracranial pressure, necessitating immediate imaging and potential surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Acute focal ataxia is commonly due to ischemic infarction or cerebellar hemorrhage.
Lesions produce ipsilateral cerebellar symptoms and may cause impaired consciousness from brainstem compression.
Consider posterior fossa subdural hematoma, bacterial abscess, or primary/metastatic cerebellar disease in worsening acute ataxia cases.
CT or MRI is essential for identifying clinically significant processes.
Sudden herniation (rostral through the tentorium or caudal of cerebellar tonsils) can be devastating; surgical decompression may be required.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · FOCAL ATAXIA | THE INHERITED ATAXIAS

items.title: Inherited Ataxias: Understanding Genetic and Clinical Diversity
items.content: Inherited ataxias exhibit diverse inheritance patterns, including autosomal dominant, recessive, or mitochondrial. They can present with a range of clinical manifestations affecting not only the cerebellum but also other neural structures.
keyPoints: Inherited ataxias may follow autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or maternal (mitochondrial) inheritance patterns.
Genomic classification has largely replaced classifications based solely on clinical expression.
Clinical manifestations can involve the cerebellum, basal ganglia, brainstem, spinal cord, optic nerves, retina, and peripheral nerves.
In large families with dominantly inherited ataxias, presentations range from purely cerebellar to mixed cerebellar and other neural disorders.
Rarely, dementia may be present in these conditions.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · FOCAL ATAXIA | THE INHERITED ATAXIAS

items.title: SCA1 Pathophysiology: Toxic Gain of Function from Expanded Polyglutamine Proteins
items.content: SCA1 results from CAG triplet repeat expansions in the ATXN1 gene, leading to toxic gain-of-function effects due to expanded polyglutamine proteins. These proteins accumulate abnormally and disrupt cellular functions.
keyPoints: CAG triplet repeat expansion causes SCA1
Expanded polyglutamine proteins (ataxins) lead to neuronal toxicity
~40 glutamines or more are potentially toxic
Mechanisms include abnormal protein conformation, impaired transcriptional regulation, and disrupted proteasome function
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.title: Clinical Presentation of SCA1: Progressive Cerebellar Ataxia with Extrapiramidal Features
items.content: SCA1 typically presents in early to middle adulthood with progressive cerebellar ataxia, including trunk and limb involvement, gait impairment, and nystagmoid eye movements. Extrapiramidal symptoms such as rigidity and parkinsonian tremor may also occur.
keyPoints: Progressive cerebellar ataxia of trunk and limbs
Impairment of equilibrium and gait
Scanning speech, nystagmoid eye movements, oscillatory tremor
Extrapiramidal symptoms: rigidity, parkinsonian tremor
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.title: Imaging and Pathological Findings in SCA1: MRI and Postmortem Observations
items.content: MRI reveals cerebellar and brainstem atrophy in SCA1. Postmortem examination shows shrinkage of the pons, loss of Purkinje cells, and degenerative changes in various CNS regions.
keyPoints: MRI shows cerebellar and brainstem atrophy
Postmortem: ventral pons shrinkage, olivary eminence disappearance
Loss of Purkinje cells, demyelination in middle cerebellar peduncle
Degeneration in CNS including posterior columns and spinocerebellar fibers
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.title: Genetic Basis and Clinical Variability of SCA2
items.content: SCA2 is characterized by CAG repeat expansions in the ataxin-2 gene, leading to a polyglutamine-containing protein. The number of repeats ranges from 35–77 in mutant alleles, compared to 15–32 in normal alleles. This genetic variation contributes to clinical variability and onset age ranging from 2 to 65 years.
keyPoints: SCA2 involves CAG repeat expansions coding for polyglutamine-containing ataxin-2.
Normal alleles have 15–32 repeats; mutant alleles have 35–77 repeats.
Clinical variability is significant, with onset age ranging from 2 to 65 years.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.title: Machado-Joseph Disease (MJD) Clinical Types and Progression
items.content: MJD is classified into three clinical types: Type I involves early-onset ALS-parkinsonism-dystonia, Type II presents with cerebellar deficits in the second to fourth decades, and Type III appears later with pancerebellar disorder. The mean age of onset is 25 years, with progression leading to death within 15 years.
keyPoints: MJD has three clinical types: I (ALS-parkinsonism-dystonia), II (ataxic type), III (ataxic-amyotrophic).
Mean age of onset is 25 years.
Neurologic deficits progress, leading to death within 15 years, especially in Types I and II.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.title: Pathologic Findings in Machado-Joseph Disease
items.content: MJD is characterized by variable neuronal loss and glial replacement in the corpus striatum, severe neuron loss in the substantia nigra's pars compacta, and Purkinje cell loss in the cerebellar cortex. Sparing of the inferior olives distinguishes it from other inherited ataxias.
keyPoints: Pathologic findings include neuronal loss in the corpus striatum and substantia nigra.
Purkinje cell loss occurs in the cerebellar cortex.
Sparing of the inferior olives is a distinguishing feature.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.title: SCA6: CAG Repeat Expansions and Clinical Variability
items.content: SCA6 is caused by CAG repeat expansions in the CACNA1A gene, which encodes the $ \alpha_{1A} $ voltage-dependent calcium channel subunit. These expansions lead to late-onset progressive ataxia with cerebellar degeneration.
keyPoints: CAG repeat expansions (21–27) in SCA6 patients; normal range is 4–16 triplets.
Different mutations in CACNA1A result in varied clinical disorders: SCA6, familial hemiplegic migraine, and hereditary paroxysmal cerebellar ataxia.
Some patients with familial hemiplegic migraine develop progressive ataxia and cerebellar atrophy.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA6 | SCA7

items.title: SCA7: CAG Repeat Expansion and Retinal Degeneration
items.content: SCA7 is characterized by an expanded CAG repeat in the gene at 3p14-p21.1, leading to retinal pigmentary degeneration and variable clinical severity.
keyPoints: Expanded CAG repeats cause SCA7 with high variability in disease severity.
Retinal abnormalities start as blue-yellow color blindness and progress to macular degeneration.
Marked anticipation is observed, especially with paternal transmission.
Ataxin-7 aggregates in nuclei of affected neurons.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA6 | SCA7

items.title: Understanding SCA8: Maternal Bias and RNA Toxicity Mechanism
items.content: SCA8 is an ataxic disorder caused by a CTG repeat expansion in chromosome 13q21, with symptoms typically starting around age 40. The condition exhibits marked maternal bias due to repeat contractions during spermatogenesis, leading to reduced penetrance.
keyPoints: CTG repeat expansion on chromosome 13q21
Symptoms begin at ~40 years of age (range: 20-65)
Marked maternal transmission bias
MRI shows cerebellar atrophy
Possible RNA-level toxic mechanism similar to myotonic dystrophy
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · SCA8 | SCA27B | DENTATORUBROPALLIDOLUYSIAN ATROPHY

items.title: DRPLA: Variable Presentation and Genetic Anticipation
items.content: DRPLA is characterized by variable symptoms including ataxia, choreoathetosis, dystonia, seizures, myoclonus, and dementia. It results from CAG triplet repeats in the atrophin gene on chromosome 12p12-ter. Larger expansions correlate with earlier onset, and anticipation occurs with paternal inheritance.
keyPoints: CAG repeat expansion in atrophin gene (chromosome 12p12-ter)
Normal individuals have ≤26 repeats; DRPLA patients have 49 repeats
Earlier onset associated with larger CAG expansions
Anticipation observed, especially with paternal inheritance
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · SCA8 | SCA27B | DENTATORUBROPALLIDOLUYSIAN ATROPHY

items.title: Understanding Episodic Ataxia Types 1 and 2: Genetic Causes and Management
items.content: Episodic ataxia type 1 (EA-1) is linked to a mutation in the KCNA1 gene on chromosome 12p, while EA-2 involves mutations in the CACNA1A gene. EA-1 episodes are brief and triggered by startle or posture changes, whereas EA-2 episodes last longer and can be induced by stress or fatigue. Acetazolamide is a common therapeutic agent for both types.
keyPoints: EA-1 linked to KCNA1 gene mutation (Phe249Le) on chromosome 12p
EA-2 associated with CACNA1A gene mutations causing stop codon, nonsense mutations
EA-1 episodes: brief ataxia with myokymia and nystagmus, triggered by startle or posture changes
EA-2 episodes: longer-lasting ataxia with nystagmus, induced by stress or fatigue
Acetazolamide can be therapeutic for both EA-1 and EA-2
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · EPISODIC ATAXIA | AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE ATAXIAS

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Friedreich's Ataxia
items.content: Friedreich's ataxia, the most common inherited ataxia, presents before age 25 with progressive gait issues and is often associated with cardiomyopathy. Neurological signs include nystagmus, dysmetria, and loss of proprioception. Cardiac involvement occurs in 90% of patients, and diabetes mellitus is present in 20%. MRI shows spinal cord atrophy.
keyPoints: Most common inherited ataxia, comprising one-half of all hereditary cases
Symptoms: progressive gait issues, dysarthria, scoliosis, cardiopathy
Neurological signs: nystagmus, loss of fast saccadic eye movements, truncal titubation
Cardiac involvement in 90% of patients; diabetes mellitus incidence at 20%
Median age of death is 35 years; MRI shows spinal cord atrophy
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · EPISODIC ATAXIA | AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE ATAXIAS

items.title: Friedreich's Ataxia Genetic Mechanism and Clinical Variability
items.content: Friedreich's ataxia is primarily caused by homozygous GAA triplet repeat expansions in the frataxin gene, leading to reduced frataxin mRNA levels. This results in mitochondrial dysfunction due to iron accumulation. Compound heterozygotes with one expanded GAA repeat and a point mutation exhibit milder symptoms.
keyPoints: Classic Friedreich's ataxia mapped to 9q13-q21.1
>95% of patients have homozygous expanded GAA repeats (200–900 repeats)
Normal individuals have 7–22 GAA repeats
Milder phenotype in compound heterozygotes with point mutation in amino-terminal half of frataxin
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Vitamin E Deficiency and Hereditary Ataxia
items.content: Hereditary ataxias related to vitamin E deficiency include abetalipoproteinemia and AVED. Abetalipoproteinemia results from mutations in MTP, impairing VLDL formation and secretion, while AVED is due to mutations in the α-tocopherol transfer protein affecting vitamin E binding.
keyPoints: Abetalipoproteinemia caused by mutations in MTP gene
AVED due to mutations in α-tocopherol transfer protein (α-TTP)
Both conditions lead to ataxic syndrome through impaired vitamin E delivery
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: CANVAS Syndrome and Ataxia Telangiectasia Clinical Features
items.content: CANVAS syndrome is caused by AAGGG repeat expansions in the RFC1 gene, often presenting with sensory neuronopathy and vestibular areflexia. Ataxia telangiectasia presents with cerebellar dysfunction, immunodeficiencies, and increased cancer risk.
keyPoints: CANVAS syndrome due to biallelic intronic AAGGG repeat expansions in RFC1
Ataxia telangiectasia (AT) presents with progressive telangiectatic lesions, cerebellar deficits, and immunodeficiency
Increased incidence of lymphomas, Hodgkin's disease, acute T-cell leukemias, and breast cancer in AT
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.title: Early Diagnosis and Risk Identification in ATM Gene Deficiency
items.content: The ATM gene at 11q22-23 is crucial for DNA repair, mitogenic signal transduction, meiotic recombination, and cell cycle control. Its identification allows early diagnosis of AT and risk assessment for associated cancers like breast cancer.
keyPoints: ATM gene located at 11q22-23
Defective DNA repair in AT fibroblasts exposed to ultraviolet light
Early diagnosis permits identification of heterozygotes at risk for cancer (e.g., breast cancer)
Elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein and immunoglobulin deficiency noted
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.title: Management of Mitochondrial Ataxia through Genetic Insight
items.content: Mitochondrial DNA mutations, including 30 pathogenic point mutations and 60 deletions, are linked to ataxia. Identifying these genetic changes aids in diagnosing mitochondrial ataxias.
keyPoints: Thirty pathogenic mtDNA point mutations known
Sixty different types of mtDNA deletions identified
Several mutations cause or are associated with ataxia
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.title: Global Prevalence and Genetic Markers in Ataxias
items.content: Ataxias, including MJD (SCA3) and Friedreich's ataxia, are globally prevalent. Genetic markers facilitate precise mutation identification for diagnosis and family planning.
keyPoints: MJD (SCA3) is the most common autosomal dominant type
Friedreich's ataxia is the most common autosomal recessive type
Genetic markers available for identifying mutations
Early detection can reduce inherited ataxia incidence
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

Development of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
items.explanation: Untreated posterior fossa lesions can lead to sudden herniation, which is usually devastating.

items.statement: Focal ataxia is always associated with chronic etiologies.
items.explanation: Focal ataxia can result from acute causes like cerebrovascular disease, not just chronic etiologies.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · FOCAL ATAXIA | THE INHERITED ATAXIAS

items.statement: SCA1 is associated with extrapyramidal symptoms such as rigidity.
items.explanation: Extrapiramidal symptoms including rigidity can occur in SCA1.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.statement: SCA1 is caused by a CTG triplet repeat expansion.
items.explanation: SCA1 is caused by a CAG triplet repeat expansion, not CTG.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.statement: Ataxin-1 aggregation is necessary for cell death in SCA1.
items.explanation: Cell death in SCA1 requires leucine-rich acidic nuclear protein localization, not ataxin-1 aggregation.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.statement: Ataxin-2 is a risk factor for sporadic amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
items.explanation: Ataxin-2 has been shown to be an important risk factor for sporadic ALS.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.statement: SCA7 is distinguished from other SCAs by the presence of retinal pigmentary degeneration.
items.explanation: SCA7 uniquely features retinal pigmentary degeneration among SCAs.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA6 | SCA7

items.statement: The CAG repeat size in the SCA7 gene is consistent and does not vary.
items.explanation: The expanded repeat size in SCA7 is highly variable, affecting disease severity.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA6 | SCA7

items.statement: Cognitive impairment is common in SCA27B.
items.explanation: Cognitive impairment is infrequent in SCA27B.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · SCA8 | SCA27B | DENTATORUBROPALLIDOLUYSIAN ATROPHY

items.explanation: DRPLA presentation may include dementia.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · SCA8 | SCA27B | DENTATORUBROPALLIDOLUYSIAN ATROPHY

items.statement: Friedreich's Ataxia is the most common form of inherited ataxia.
items.explanation: Friedreich's Ataxia composes one-half of all hereditary ataxias.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · EPISODIC ATAXIA | AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE ATAXIAS

items.statement: The median age of death for patients with Friedreich's Ataxia is 50 years.
items.explanation: The median age of death in Friedreich's Ataxia is 35 years.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · EPISODIC ATAXIA | AUTOSOMAL RECESSIVE ATAXIAS

items.statement: Patients with Friedreich's ataxia have normal levels of frataxin mRNA.
items.explanation: Patients have undetectable or extremely low levels of frataxin mRNA.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: AVED is caused by mutations in the gene coding for microsomal triglyceride transfer protein.
items.explanation: AVED is due to mutations in the gene for α-tocopherol transfer protein.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.statement: There is a proven therapy for all autosomal dominant ataxias.
items.explanation: There is no proven therapy for any of the autosomal dominant ataxias (SCA1 to SCA43).
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.statement: Genetic markers are available for precise identification of mutations in ataxia.
items.explanation: Genetic markers are now commercially available to precisely identify the genetic mutation for correct diagnosis.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA
items.assertion: Autoantibody-associated cerebellar syndromes can occur without cancer.
items.reason: The most common is a progressive ataxic syndrome associated with serum anti-glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD65) antibodies.
items.explanation: Autoantibody-associated cerebellar syndromes, such as those involving GAD65 antibodies, can occur without an association with cancer.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA

items.subtopic: Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA
items.assertion: Prion disorders are a common cause of acute ataxia.
items.reason: Subacute progressive ataxia may be caused by prion disorders, especially with infectious etiology like contaminated human growth hormone.
items.explanation: Prion disorders typically cause subacute or chronic ataxia, not acute ataxia.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · Ataxic Disorders | Ataxic Disorders | SYMMETRIC ATAXIA

items.assertion: Inherited ataxias may show a variety of clinical manifestations within the same family.
items.reason: Large families with dominantly inherited ataxias can exhibit gradations from purely cerebellar to mixed or entirely different phenotypes.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains the variability in clinical manifestations within families.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · FOCAL ATAXIA | THE INHERITED ATAXIAS

items.assertion: The most common symptom of SCA1 is cerebellar ataxia.
items.reason: SCA1 presents with progressive cerebellar ataxia of the trunk and limbs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.assertion: SCA1 is characterized by a loss of Purkinje cells.
items.reason: Histologic examination shows variable loss of Purkinje cells in SCA1.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT ATAXIAS | SCA1

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.assertion: The mean age of onset for symptoms in Machado-Joseph Disease is 25 years.
items.reason: Neurologic deficits invariably progress and lead to death from debilitation within 15 years of onset, especially in patients with types I and II disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion about the mean age of onset and the reason regarding progression are true, but the reason is not an explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3
items.assertion: Juvenile patients with SCA1 have fewer CAG repeats than adult-onset cases.
items.reason: There is a direct correlation between the number of CAG repeats and the age of onset for SCA1.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; juvenile patients actually have higher numbers of CAG repeats.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | SCA2 | GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MACHADO-JOSEPH DISEASE/SCA3

items.assertion: Anticipation in DRPLA is associated with larger CAG repeat expansions.
items.reason: Earlier onset of disease occurs with an increasing CAG repeat number when inherited from the father.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the anticipation phenomenon in DRPLA.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · SCA8 | SCA27B | DENTATORUBROPALLIDOLUYSIAN ATROPHY

items.assertion: Loss of frataxin expression leads to mitochondrial iron accumulation.
items.reason: Frataxin is involved in iron homeostasis, and its deficiency results in mitochondrial iron accumulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Abetalipoproteinemia results from impaired binding of vitamin E to VLDL.
items.reason: Defects in MTP result in impairment of formation and secretion of VLDL, affecting vitamin E delivery.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because abetalipoproteinemia results from absence of VLDL, not impaired binding.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.assertion: Acetazolamide can reduce the duration of symptoms in EA.
items.reason: EA is an ataxia that benefits from acetazolamide treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES

items.subtopic: GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
items.assertion: Iron chelators are beneficial in Friedrich's ataxia.
items.reason: They address mitochondrial dysfunction by reducing iron overload.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because iron chelators are potentially harmful in Friedrich's patients.
items.reference: Ataxic Disorders · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | MITOCHONDRIAL ATAXIA | Ataxic Disorders | GENETIC DIAGNOSTIC LABORATORIES | GLOBAL FEATURES
